summary: It's been a wild day across the neighborhoods, with Reynoldstown finally seeing that long-stalled BeltLine development break ground while Decatur gets ready for some big community meetings. Plus Lewis Barbecue just opened at Ansley Mall with those gorgeous views of the BeltLine bridge.

topStory.headline: Reynoldstown's Long-Stalled BeltLine Corner Finally Breaks Ground
topStory.summary: That empty lot on Chester Avenue that's been sitting there for nearly five years? Construction is finally happening. Embry Development took over from Toll Brothers and is building 85 residential units just a block from the Eastside Trail. It's called Camber Crossing now, and locals are relieved to see progress on what had become a neighborhood eyesore.
topStory.body:
You know that cleared corner where Chester Avenue meets Fulton Terrace in Reynoldstown? The one that's been sitting empty since 2021, making everyone wonder what the heck happened to the promised development? Well, grab your hard hats because construction is finally underway.

Embry Development Company confirmed they've started groundwork on what's now called Camber Crossing, taking over the project from national developer Toll Brothers who had let it sit dormant. The Atlanta-based company bought the 2-acre site two years ago with plans for 85 residential units, and they're delivering a mix of townhomes and for-sale condos about a block west of the ever-popular Eastside Trail.

For neighbors who've been staring at this vacant lot for years (you can practically see the BeltLine from there), this is huge news. The development sits right in the sweet spot of Memorial Drive accessibility and BeltLine proximity that makes Reynoldstown such a hot commodity. No word yet on pricing, but given the location and market, expect these units to move fast once they're ready.
topStory.imageAlt: Construction underway at Camber Crossing development in Reynoldstown

Embry Development Company confirmed they've started groundwork on what's now called Camber Crossing, taking over the project from national developer Toll Brothers who had let it sit dormant. stories.headline: Decatur's Missing Middle Housing Gets Real at Cottages Midway
stories.summary: With Decatur home prices hitting $700k, this village-style development on Midway Road is trying to create something more accessible. Eighteen standalone houses around shared courtyards and greenspace, soft-launching next week. It's the kind of thoughtful infill development that could help with our housing crunch.
stories.body:
Here's some potentially good news for anyone who's been priced out of Decatur proper: Cottages at Midway is about to soft-launch, and it's exactly the kind of 'missing middle' housing we've been talking about needing more of.

The 18 standalone houses are going up on 2.1 acres at 2883 and 2893 Midway Road, replacing two underused lots with a village-style community built around shared courtyards and greenspace. The developers are calling it mixed-income, which in today's market usually means 'still expensive but not completely insane.' Given that median home values in Decatur have climbed past $700,000, even moderately expensive feels like a win.

What I love about this project is the intentional community design. Instead of cramming as many units as possible onto the lots, they're creating actual gathering spaces and preserving green areas. It's near a major commercial corridor and downtown Decatur, so you get walkability without sacrificing the neighborhood feel that makes this area special. The soft launch starts next week, so we'll see how the market responds to this approach.
stories.imageAlt: Site plan for Cottages at Midway development in Decatur

stories.headline: West End's BeltLine Townhomes Start Taking Shape
stories.summary: GW Parkside (formerly Hopkins and White Townhomes) is finally rising up along the BeltLine near Lee + White. Ackerman & Co. is bringing 23 for-sale townhomes to market this spring, and the first seven are almost ready. It's rare to see new for-sale housing along this popular Southwest BeltLine stretch.
stories.body:
If you've been walking the Southwest BeltLine near Lee + White lately, you've probably noticed construction happening at 600 Hopkins Street. That's GW Parkside (they renamed it from Hopkins and White Townhomes), and it's starting to look like actual buildings instead of just dirt piles.

Ackerman & Co., the same developer behind Lee + White's food and office scene, started demo on this project a year ago and now has 23 for-sale townhomes in the works. The first seven units are nearing completion and should hit the market this spring, which is exciting news for anyone wanting to own instead of rent along this stretch of the BeltLine.

What makes this project interesting is how rare new for-sale housing has become along the Southwest BeltLine corridor. Most of what we've seen lately has been rental apartments, so having townhomes for purchase gives people a different path to BeltLine living. Plus, with Ackerman's track record at Lee + White, there's reason to be optimistic about the quality and community integration.
stories.imageAlt: Rendering of GW Parkside townhomes in West End

stories.headline: Lewis Barbecue Brings Austin-Style Smoke to the BeltLine
stories.summary: The legendary pitmaster John Lewis just opened at Ansley Mall with six massive smokers running 24/7 in a gorgeous loft overlooking the new BeltLine bridge. This isn't just good barbecue, it's some of the best brisket you'll find in Atlanta, served in what might be Georgia's fanciest smokehouse.
stories.body:
Forget everything you think you know about barbecue restaurants, because Lewis Barbecue at Ansley Mall is operating on a completely different level. John Lewis has created what might be the most spectacular smokehouse in Georgia, with six 22-foot smokers running around the clock in a spacious loft with panoramic windows overlooking the new pedestrian bridge to the BeltLine.

Lewis, who helped weld those massive smokers from 1,000-gallon propane tanks, has brought his Austin expertise (by way of Charleston) to create what the reviewer calls some of the best brisket in Atlanta. The operation runs like a factory, with smokers staggered in eight-hour shifts to maintain consistent quality. It's the kind of serious barbecue operation that Austin and Kansas City are known for, now right here on our doorstep.

The location behind The Cook's Warehouse couldn't be better for BeltLine access, and those upstairs windows create an atmosphere unlike any other barbecue joint in town. This is destination-level barbecue with the views to match. If you're serious about smoked meat, this needs to be on your list immediately.
stories.imageAlt: Loaded barbecue platter at Lewis Barbecue

stories.headline: Mushi Ni Brings Asian-American Comfort Food to Inman Park
stories.summary: Chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez opened their first restaurant today on Elizabeth Street, just off the Freedom Park Trail. Expect soft shell crab bao, Peking chicken platters, and Thai tea martinis in a spot that evolved from their successful pop-up. It's the kind of neighborhood gem Inman Park does so well.
stories.body:
Inman Park just got a delicious new neighbor as Mushi Ni opened its doors today on Elizabeth Street, right off the Freedom Park Trail. This is the first brick-and-mortar spot for chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez, who've been building a following through their pop-up work around town.

The menu is all about Asian-American comfort food, from soft shell crab bao and chicken longanisa to umami fries and crab Rangoon puffs. Their signature Peking chicken platter feeds two people for just $25, which feels like a steal in today's restaurant landscape. The cocktail program includes creative drinks like tamarind whiskey sours and Thai tea martinis that sound perfectly suited to the neighborhood's adventurous palate.

What I love about this opening is how it represents the natural evolution from pop-up to permanent spot. Le worked at some serious kitchens (including time with Gordon Ramsay in NYC for Jimenez), and now they're bringing that experience to a location that's perfectly positioned for both foot traffic from the trail and the kind of loyal neighborhood following that Inman Park restaurants thrive on.
stories.imageAlt: Peking chicken platter at Mushi Ni restaurant

stories.headline: Eastside Gets New Food Hall From Gansevoort Market Creator
stories.summary: Robert Montwaid, the guy behind NYC's famous Gansevoort Market, is opening Vela Park Food Works near East Lake Golf Club in mid-April. Six different chef concepts under one roof with craft cocktails and outdoor dining. It's his third Atlanta food destination, and the eastside has been waiting for something like this.
stories.body:
The eastside food scene is about to get a major upgrade with Vela Park Food Works, set to open in mid-April near East Lake Golf Club. This is the third Atlanta project from Robert Montwaid, who created the legendary Gansevoort Market in New York City, so expectations are running high.

The food hall will feature six different chef-driven concepts under one roof, with craft cocktails and extensive outdoor dining space. It's part of the larger Vela Park rental community, giving the area a centralized dining destination that's been missing from this part of town. The eastside has been underserved compared to the BeltLine corridor developments, so having a quality food hall fills a real gap.

Montwaid knows how to curate food concepts that work together while maintaining their individual identities. His track record with Atlanta food destinations gives this project credibility, and the timing feels perfect as more people discover the East Lake area. Mid-April can't come soon enough for eastside food lovers who've been making the trek to other neighborhoods for this kind of dining variety.
stories.imageAlt: Interior rendering of Vela Park Food Works

stories.headline: Decatur's West Howard Complete Streets Makeover Nearly Done
stories.summary: That drag strip of a road between downtown Decatur and East Lake MARTA is finally getting civilized. The bike lanes with concrete barriers, new sidewalks, and traffic calming measures are almost complete after nearly a decade of planning. It's exactly the kind of infrastructure that makes neighborhoods more walkable.
stories.body:
After nearly a decade of planning and months of construction, Decatur's transformation of West Howard Avenue from a high-speed motorist route into a people-friendly Complete Street is almost finished. The roughly one-mile stretch between downtown Decatur and East Lake MARTA station now features protected bike lanes, upgraded sidewalks, and serious traffic calming measures.

The project includes a two-way cycle track with concrete barriers for most of the route, running from Commerce Drive near Decatur High School to the city limits at Paden Circle. It's the kind of infrastructure investment that actually makes neighborhoods more walkable and bikeable instead of just talking about it. The timing is perfect with all the residential development happening along this corridor.

What's great about this project is how it connects existing infrastructure, linking downtown Decatur directly to East Lake MARTA and creating a safer route for people who want to get around without a car. The concrete barriers show they're serious about protecting cyclists, not just painting some lines and calling it bike infrastructure. This is what real Complete Streets implementation looks like.
stories.imageAlt: Protected bike lane with concrete barriers on West Howard Avenue in Decatur

stories.headline: City Council Advances ICE Controls While Fire Union Fights Continue
stories.summary: The Public Safety Committee unanimously moved forward resolutions to ban ICE detention facilities and require documentation of ICE activities during police operations. But dozens of Atlantans also showed up to blast Mayor Dickens for refusing to sign the firefighters' union contract that council already ratified.
stories.body:
Atlanta City Council's Public Safety Committee had a busy day Monday, unanimously advancing two resolutions aimed at controlling ICE activities in the city while also hearing an earful about Mayor Dickens' ongoing standoff with the firefighters union.

The ICE-related measures would ban Immigration and Customs Enforcement detention facilities within city limits and require Atlanta police to document any ICE agent activities when they're present during police operations. Both resolutions head to the full council for an April 20 vote, and given the committee's unanimous support, they're likely to pass.

But the bigger drama came from dozens of Atlantans who showed up to criticize Dickens for refusing to sign the city's first-ever firefighter union contract, which the council ratified back in April 2025. The International Association of Fire Fighters has been pushing for the mayor to follow through on what council already approved, creating an unusual political standoff where the legislative and executive branches are at odds over labor relations.
stories.imageAlt: Atlanta City Council Public Safety Committee meeting

stories.headline: DeKalb Parents Protest School Closure Plans at Lakeside
stories.summary: Hundreds of frustrated parents showed up at Lakeside High to voice opposition to DeKalb's latest school closure proposal. Oak Grove and Evansdale families took to the streets outside, calling the process a farce and demanding a student-first approach to redistricting.
stories.body:
The DeKalb County Schools closure saga reached a boiling point Monday night as hundreds of angry parents packed Lakeside High School for the latest public input session. The scene outside was equally intense, with Oak Grove and Evansdale families protesting what they're calling a sham process that ignores community input.

Parents held signs advocating for small schools and a people-first approach to the Student Assignment Project, with many saying they feel completely shut out of decisions that will fundamentally change their children's educational experience. The frustration is palpable, as Samuel Howe from Oak Grove put it: 'This is not a process. This is a farce, and they're not listening.'

What's particularly galling for parents is that this is round two of the closure process, after the first attempt fell apart under community pressure. Now families are facing the same upheaval with what they see as the same lack of meaningful input. The district says it's listening, but the protests suggest a massive disconnect between what officials think they're providing and what families actually need to feel heard.
stories.imageAlt: Parents and children protesting DeKalb school closures outside Lakeside High School

stories.headline: Seven-Year-Old Dies in Accidental Shooting in Ellenwood
stories.summary: A tragic accident overnight in DeKalb County as a 7-year-old boy died after getting hold of a gun and shooting himself. The family is devastated, and it's another heartbreaking reminder of the importance of gun safety in homes with children.
stories.body:
A heartbreaking tragedy unfolded overnight in DeKalb County as a 7-year-old boy died in what family members are calling an accidental shooting. Police responded to a home on Loveless Drive in the Ellenwood area, where they found the child deceased inside the residence.

Family members at the scene told reporters that the boy had gotten hold of a gun and shot himself. Police investigators are treating this as an 'unintended discharge of a weapon' while they work to confirm the exact circumstances. The investigation drew a large crowd of grieving family and friends to the neighborhood, with Loveless Drive blocked off for several hours.

This is every parent's worst nightmare, and it underscores the critical importance of proper gun storage in homes with children. While the investigation continues, the immediate focus is on supporting a family that's dealing with unimaginable loss. These tragedies are entirely preventable with proper safety measures, but that doesn't make the grief any easier for those left behind.
stories.imageAlt: Police cars at scene of investigation on Loveless Drive

stories.headline: Man Killed Outside Vine City Food Mart
stories.summary: A deadly shooting at a market on Joseph E. Boone Boulevard Tuesday night has left one man dead. Atlanta police are investigating what led to the gunfire, and it's yet another reminder of the violence affecting our neighborhoods.
stories.body:
Atlanta police are investigating a fatal shooting that occurred Tuesday evening outside a food mart in the Vine City neighborhood. The incident happened just before 9 p.m. on Joseph E. Boone Boulevard NW, near Joseph E. Lowery Boulevard.

Officers arrived to find a man who had been shot and immediately rushed him to a local hospital, where he later died from his injuries. Police have not yet released the victim's identity or provided details about potential suspects or what may have led to the shooting.

This tragic incident adds to ongoing concerns about violence in Atlanta neighborhoods, particularly around commercial areas that should be safe spaces for community members. The investigation is ongoing, and police are working to determine the circumstances that led to this senseless loss of life.
stories.imageAlt: Police investigating shooting scene on Joseph E. Boone Boulevard

stories.headline: Eight-Foot Python Shows Up on the Atlanta BeltLine
stories.summary: Someone's pet python made quite the scene on the BeltLine when a man was filmed yanking the 8-to-10-foot snake out of the bushes. Wildlife officials are warning people not to handle random animals they find, even if they think they're helping.
stories.body:
The Atlanta BeltLine has seen its share of wildlife, but an 8-to-10-foot python definitely wasn't on anyone's bingo card for this year. Video captured by Joseph Ragland shows a man handling the massive snake after pulling it from bushes along the trail, creating quite the spectacle for other BeltLine users.

Ragland was trying to get a better look at what he knew wasn't your typical Georgia snake when 'somebody's uncle comes out of nowhere and starts yanking it out of the bush.' The result was 10 feet of snake laid out on the BeltLine, drawing crowds and raising serious safety concerns from wildlife experts.

Jason Clark from Southeastern Reptile Rescue called the video 'very concerning,' noting that handling unknown animals is dangerous for both the person and the animal. It's unclear whether the man in the video was the owner or just a good Samaritan, but wildlife officials are using this as a teaching moment: if you see exotic animals on the BeltLine (or anywhere else), call the professionals instead of trying to wrestle them yourself.
stories.imageAlt: Man handling large python on Atlanta BeltLine
